# 10 Best Blenders Under $100 for 2026
[Leave a Comment](https://blenderjunkie.com/best-blender-for-under-100-dollars/#respond) / By  [ Elijah Okoro  ](https://blenderjunkie.com/author/elijahokoro/ "View all posts by Elijah Okoro") /  April 11, 2026 
We’ve narrowed down ten blenders that cost under $100 and still deliver solid performance for 2026. The list mixes compact **250 W Magic Bullet** sets, a **1000 W Ninja Pro** for families, a versatile 700 W Rexyhome, and space‑saving Bella 450 W models, plus a portable 1300 W Ganiza and a few hand‑held options. All feature **BPA‑free jars** , dishwasher‑safe parts, multiple speeds, and safety interlocks. We’ll compare motor power, blade durability, jar size, and warranty to help you pick the best fit.

More Details on Our Top Picks
  1. ## Magic Bullet Blender Small Silver 11 Piece Set
[![Magic Bullet Blender Small Silver 11 Piece Set](https://m.media-amazon.com/images/I/41BKzSi-ifL._SL500_.jpg)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B012T634SM?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
Compact Powerhouse
[View Latest Price](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B012T634SM?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
If you need a **compact blender** that fits a dorm or a tiny kitchen, the Magic Bullet Small, Silver, 11‑piece set is a solid pick. We like its **250W motor** and **six‑speed push button**. The set includes a cross‑blade, tall and short cups, a party mug, two lip rings, two Stay‑Fresh lids, a to‑go lid, and a recipe guide. It chops, mixes, blends, whips, grinds, and minces in seconds. The cyclonic cutting zone boosts efficiency. All parts are **dishwasher‑safe** and BPA‑free. It weighs 3.3 lb and fits on a small counter. A five‑year limited warranty backs it.
     * Power (Wattage):250 W
     * Capacity (Jar/ Pitcher Size):11‑piece set (tall cup, short cup, party mug) – max ≈ 12 oz each
     * Speed Settings:6‑speed push button
     * Blade Material:Stainless steel cross‑blade
     * BPA‑Free Components:BPA‑free cups and lids
     * Warranty Length:5‑year limited
     * Additional Feature:Cyclonic Cutting Zone design
     * Additional Feature:Compact countertop footprint
     * Additional Feature:5‑year limited warranty
  2. ## Ninja Professional 1000W Blender with XL 72‑oz Pitcher
[![Ninja Professional 1000W Blender with XL 72‑oz Pitcher](https://m.media-amazon.com/images/I/31jWCOWssAL._SL500_.jpg)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00NGV4506?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
Family‑Size Performer
[View Latest Price](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00NGV4506?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
Looking for a blender that can handle a family‑size batch of smoothies without breaking the bank? We tested the **Ninja Professional 1000W Blender** with its **XL 72‑oz pitcher**. It delivers 1000 watts of power and uses **Total Crushing Technology blades** that melt ice and puree whole fruit quickly. The pitcher holds up to 64 oz of liquid, is BPA‑free, and dishwasher‑safe. We found the four‑speed settings easy to use for smoothies, juices, and frozen drinks. The stainless‑steel blades stay sharp, and the base weighs 7.1 lb, making it stable on the counter. It comes with a recipe guide and a **one‑year limited warranty**. This model fits well for family use and stays under $100.
     * Power (Wattage):1000 W
     * Capacity (Jar/ Pitcher Size):72 oz pitcher (64 oz liquid)
     * Speed Settings:4‑speed
     * Blade Material:Stainless steel Total Crushing blade
     * BPA‑Free Components:BPA‑free pitcher and lid
     * Warranty Length:1‑year limited
     * Additional Feature:Total Crushing Technology blades
     * Additional Feature:Family‑size 72‑oz pitcher
     * Additional Feature:1‑year limited warranty
  3. ## Blender 700W Countertop 50oz BPA-Free Jar 3-Speed
[![](https://blenderjunkie.com/best-blender-for-under-100-dollars/)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0GS3SKYL9?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
Large‑Batch Champion
[View Latest Price](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0GS3SKYL9?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
We’re looking for a blender that can handle big batches without breaking the bank, and the 700‑watt REXYHOME model fits that need. It offers a **50‑oz** **BPA‑free** jar that holds enough for family smoothies or sauces. The **stainless‑steel** six‑point blade crushes ice and frozen fruit without clumps. Three speed settings plus a pulse give us fine control, and the dual interlock safety stops the motor unless the jar and lid are locked. Cleaning is easy—jar and lid are **dishwasher safe**. The unit weighs just over three pounds, fits a compact countertop, and includes a one‑year warranty.
     * Power (Wattage):700 W
     * Capacity (Jar/ Pitcher Size):50 oz (1.5 L) jar
     * Speed Settings:3‑speed (plus pulse)
     * Blade Material:Stainless steel 6‑point blade
     * BPA‑Free Components:BPA‑free jar
     * Warranty Length:1‑year worry‑free
     * Additional Feature:Dual secure safety interlock
     * Additional Feature:50‑oz family batch jar
     * Additional Feature:1‑year worry‑free warranty
  4. ## Bella 10-Speed Countertop Blender & Juicer 48oz
[![Bella 10-Speed Countertop Blender & Juicer 48oz](https://m.media-amazon.com/images/I/3185TQxCO2L._SL500_.jpg)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0CYJ9YJVY?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
Space‑Saving Pro
[View Latest Price](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0CYJ9YJVY?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
The Bella 10‑Speed Countertop Blender & Juicer is perfect for **small kitchens** where space is tight and you need a **versatile tool** under $100. We like its 48 oz BPA‑free jar that **flips onto the base** , saving up to 50 % of storage space. The slim profile and built‑in cord storage keep countertops tidy. A 450‑watt motor with a 4‑tip stainless steel blade handles ice, frozen fruit, and veggies. **Ten speeds** let us crush, puree, or liquefy any texture. The jar and lid are dishwasher‑safe, and the removable measuring cap adds precision. We appreciate the two‑year warranty for peace of mind.
     * Power (Wattage):450 W
     * Capacity (Jar/ Pitcher Size):48 oz jar
     * Speed Settings:10‑speed
     * Blade Material:Stainless steel 4‑tip blade
     * BPA‑Free Components:BPA‑free jar and lid
     * Warranty Length:2‑year limited
     * Additional Feature:Flip‑jar storage saves space
     * Additional Feature:Removable measuring cap lid
     * Additional Feature:2‑year limited warranty
  5. ## Ganiza 1300W Portable Smoothie Blender with Cups
[![Ganiza 1300W Portable Smoothie Blender with Cups](https://m.media-amazon.com/images/I/51BEx6GI6JL._SL500_.jpg)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0FR9FXGZ7?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
Portable Power
[View Latest Price](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0FR9FXGZ7?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
If you need a powerful blender that fits in a backpack, the Ganiza **1300W** Portable Smoothie Blender is a solid pick. It packs a 1300W motor and a **six‑blade** stainless‑steel head. We get two 37‑oz cups and a 23‑oz cup, all **BPA‑free**. The cups lock in place and twist to blend, so we can crush ice, grind coffee, nuts, or make baby food in seconds. **Overheat protection** and vented housing keep it safe. The base is grippy, the blades are rugged, and cleaning is easy with the brush. It ships with lids, sealing rings, and a recipe guide. The 1‑year warranty backs the build.

     * Capacity (Jar/ Pitcher Size):2 × 37 oz cups + 1 × 23 oz cup

  6. ## Immersion Blender Handheld 1100W Variable Speed Set
[![Immersion Blender Handheld 1100W Variable Speed Set](https://m.media-amazon.com/images/I/41TevXHn-zL._SL500_.jpg)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0CPLVLFK2?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
Handheld Pro
[View Latest Price](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0CPLVLFK2?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
A **1100‑watt motor** makes this immersion blender a solid pick for anyone who needs fast, smooth purees without spending a lot. We like its **five‑speed push‑button control** and the infinite variable speed for fine adjustments. The **stainless‑steel blades** cut through ice and tough veggies in seconds, while the nylon guard protects pots. It comes with a whisk, frother, chopper, and a **600 ml beaker** , all easy to snap on and off. The corded design stays at 120 V, and the non‑slip grip feels comfortable during long use. We’ve seen it earn 4.5 stars from over 5,700 reviewers.

     * Capacity (Jar/ Pitcher Size):600 ml beaker + 500 ml food‑processor cup

     * Additional Feature:Infinitely variable speed trigger

  7. ## SANGCON 5-in-1 Blender & Food Processor 350W 2 Speeds
Pulse
[![SANGCON 5-in-1 Blender & Food Processor 350W 2 SpeedsPulse](https://m.media-amazon.com/images/I/510229nCovL._SL500_.jpg)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B08R9HDYM6?tag=blenderjunkie-20)
Multi‑Task Master

## Factors to Consider When Choosing a Blender for Under 100 Dollars
![power motor blades capacity](https://blenderjunkie.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/power_motor_blades_capacity_q3kro.jpg)
We’ll look at **power rating and efficiency** , motor type and durability, and **blade material and design**. We’ll also compare jar capacity and material with speed settings and controls. These factors help us pick a reliable blender that fits a $100 budget.
### Power Rating and Efficiency
Choosing a blender under $100 starts with checking its **power rating** , because wattage tells us how well it handles tough ingredients. We look for models in the **450 W to 1000 W range**. A 700‑1000 W motor usually crushes ice faster and makes smoother smoothies than a 350‑450 W unit. We focus on **sustained wattage** , not just peak numbers, because real‑world use demands consistent power. **Counter‑top blenders** tend to be more efficient per cycle than smaller immersion sticks, thanks to larger jars and sturdier blades. Higher‑wattage models often add multiple speeds or a **pulse function** , which can improve efficiency for common tasks. We still check that the budget option delivers reliable performance for everyday blending.
### Motor Type and Durability
A solid motor is the heart of any blender, and under $100 we still expect reliable performance. We should look for a motor in the **700‑1000‑watt** range. That power lets us crush ice and frozen fruit without stalling. A **copper‑wrapped motor** often lasts longer than cheaper alternatives, so we favor those when they fit the price. Check for a **safety interlock** that won’t let the jar spin unless it’s properly seated; this protects the motor from strain. We also want **appliance‑grade units** that come with at least a **one‑year warranty** , covering early burnout. Heavier bases usually mean a sturdier motor assembly, reducing wobble and stripping at high speeds. Choosing these features helps us get a durable blender that won’t quit after a few months.
### Blade Material and Design
What matters most in a budget blender is the blade’s material and shape. We look for **stainless steel** because it stays sharp and resists rust. Most cheap models use a **4‑tip or multi‑blade layout** , which helps crush ice and give smoothies a smoother texture. Some designs add **cross or multi‑angle blades** to stir up turbulence, improving blend consistency. Higher‑grade stainless steel is marketed as more durable for frequent use, but the difference is often subtle at this price point. **Detachable blades** make cleaning easier, and **dishwasher‑safe assemblies** add convenience. We prefer blades that stay attached securely during operation, yet can be removed quickly for maintenance. This balance of material strength and thoughtful design keeps performance reliable without extra cost.
### Jar Capacity and Material
We’ll see that **jar capacity** and material shape how we use a sub‑$100 blender. The capacity ranges from about 24 oz to 72 oz, so we can pick a size that fits our typical batch. Smaller jars work for single‑serve smoothies; larger ones let us blend soups or sauces without stopping. Most models use **BPA‑free plastic** , which keeps weight down and is usually dishwasher‑safe. Some offer **glass jars** , which feel sturdier and are easy to clean, though they add weight. A few kits include two jars—like a 48 oz and a 64 oz—so we can switch between small and big batches. **Stainless‑steel blades** attach directly to the jar, ensuring efficient blending. **Dishwasher‑safe lids** and jars make cleanup quick, even after blending larger volumes.
### Speed Settings and Controls
How many speeds do you really need? We look for blenders with **several speed settings** , usually **three to eight** , so we can tweak texture and tackle different ingredients. A **pulse function** is a must; it gives short bursts that let us control chunky blends and crush ice without over‑mixing. **Handheld models** often offer **variable speeds** —five is common—giving precise control when we blend soups or sauces. Countertop units may have fixed speeds, but many still include pre‑programmed modes like ice crush, smoothie, or puree for ease of use. Under $100, the best options show clear speed ranges and a reliable pulse button. Prioritize those features for versatility and consistent results.
### Noise Level and Vibration
A few things determine how loud a **budget blender** will be. **Lower‑wattage models** , around 450‑700 W, tend to run quieter than high‑power units. **Blade design** matters too; sharper, faster blades can raise the decibel level. Jar construction also plays a role. **Sealed or insulated jars** dampen sound, while open jars let noise escape. Pulse and variable‑speed controls let us use short bursts instead of continuous high speed, cutting down on noise and vibration. Some 1100 W immersion or countertop models are louder because the motor is exposed and the blades spin faster. **Inexpensive blenders** often skip sound‑dampening features, so we should expect them to be louder than mid‑range or premium options. Choosing a model with a lower wattage, insulated jar, and pulse function helps keep noise and vibration manageable.
### Cleaning and Maintenance Ease
Noise can be a hassle, but it’s not the only thing we worry about when picking a cheap blender. We should look for **dishwasher‑safe parts**. If the jar, lid, and blades go in the machine, clean‑up is quick. **BPA‑free containers** matter too; they won’t leach chemicals when we wash with hot water or detergent. Some models have a **self‑clean cycle** —just add soap and water, hit a button, and the motor does the work. Detachable blades and easy‑to‑reach seals stop food from sticking, making manual rinsing simple. Fewer parts mean fewer places for grime to hide. Finally, a clear, **spill‑resistant lid** helps us avoid messes before they start, cutting down overall cleaning time.
### Warranty and Support Coverage
What should we look for in a blender’s warranty? We need at least a **one‑year limited warranty** , and some models even give two years. Check that it covers **motor failure** , **blade wear** , and damage not caused by the user. Look for clear support hours—24/7 is great, but business‑hour help works if it’s easy to reach. Make sure the **claim process is simple** : online form, phone line, or email, and that you only need a receipt or registration. Verify that the warranty defines proper use, like dishwasher‑safe parts and safe operating conditions, so we don’t accidentally void it. Easy access to **replacement parts** also matters, because we’ll want to keep the blender running without hassle.

When shopping for a budget-friendly blender, it can feel like you need to sacrifice a lot to find something at a lower price point. Blenders under $100 tend to have simple controls and lack additional features or attachments, but they may also struggle to blend fibrous or tough ingredients, like kale or nuts, or to process hot food. Luckily, with so many blenders on the market these days, there are still several affordable options that hold their own against pricier models.
We've tested over 135 blenders, and below are our recommendations for the best blenders under $100. If you want more to consider, see our picks for the [best budget blenders](https://www.rtings.com/blender/reviews/best/budget-cheap), or check out our recommendations for the [best glass jar blenders](https://www.rtings.com/blender/reviews/best/glass) or the [best blenders for smoothies](https://www.rtings.com/blender/reviews/best/smoothies).

The best blender under $100 we've tested is the Chefman Obliterator, thanks to its strong, versatile performance across a wide variety of applications. While its price can sometimes rise a little above $100, it's often available for less, especially if you forego the 20-ounce personal jar and opt for just the 48-ounce main pitcher. Regardless of which jar you use, this blender feels impressively well-built, especially for the price, and produces a fantastically smooth blend, even with fibrous ingredients like kale or spinach, making it an excellent choice for soups and smoothies. It's also a fantastic ice crusher, and unlike many 48-ounce blenders, it can quickly crush a dozen ice cubes into snow-like ice. Thicker recipes are a bit more difficult, however. It makes exceptionally smooth nut butter, but you have to stop to scrape the jar frequently.
It also has some convenient features: its 17 speed settings give you a lot of control over the blending speed, or you can use 'Auto-Blend' to have the machine automatically adjust the blending setting and duration. The main jar can be used for hot blending and has a 2-part lid, allowing you to add ingredients mid-blend. There's also a spout lid for the personal jar so you can bring your blend on the go. With all that said, this blender is on the loud side, so it may not be the ideal choice for early-morning smoothies.

It comes with a sturdy 48-ounce glass pitcher that is more scratch- and odor-resistant than plastic. That said, the rest of the blender isn't as well-made, with some parts feeling flimsy, and the included 24-ounce personal jar is made of plastic. Using either jar yields a mostly smooth blend with fibrous ingredients like kale, making it a great option for soup and smoothies. It also makes smooth, spreadable nut butter, although it's not the fastest, and you must stop the blender and stir. However, it's not ideal for crushing ice, since even with a small batch of about six cubes, it leaves some unprocessed pieces.
It has a few automatic blending programs, so you don't have to stop the blender manually. If you like to make frozen treats like slushies or large batches of blended drinks, such as margaritas, you might need a blender that can handle more than the [Black+Decker Quiet Blender with Cyclone Glass Jar](https://www.rtings.com/blender/reviews/black-decker/quiet-blender-with-cyclone-glass-jar). Enter the Ninja Professional Blender 1000, which comes with a 72-ounce pitcher and pulverizes a large batch of ice cubes with ease. Its sturdy design is particularly important if you use your blender often for tough jobs like crushing ice. It doesn't have a 2-part lid, so you can't add ingredients mid-blend, but it has a pouring spout to help serve drinks from the pitcher. Although it still does a good job in small batches, it doesn't completely blend fibrous ingredients like kale, so recipes with fruit, like blueberries or pineapple, will have some unprocessed bits left over.
Unfortunately, it can't blend hot ingredients and only comes with one jar, so there's no personal jar for single-serve smoothies. Apart from a 'Pulse' button, it lacks automatic blending programs, so you have to manually switch off the blender when your recipe is finished. It's also tough to clean; the blades are removable, but sharp, and there are some tight spots that are difficult to reach.

If you mainly use your blender for smoothies, you might prefer the nutribullet Blender Combo. While this blender can retail for more than $100, you can often find it on sale for much less. It comes with a large-capacity 64-ounce jar for multiple servings and a pair of 32-ounce jars for single servings. Plus, nutribullet sells extra accessories for its blenders, so you can always purchase more personal jars if a few people in your household like smoothies in the morning. It creates an incredibly smooth blend with fibrous ingredients in any of its jars, so it'll make well-blended smoothies with common ingredients like fruit and leafy greens.
However, its lowest blending speed is still quite fast, and the blender doesn't have a tamper, so it's not ideal for thicker smoothie bowls or nut butter. And while it can handle a couple of ice cubes in a smoothie, it's not meant for crushing ice on its own. Still, the nutribullet is worth considering if you're looking for a relatively inexpensive blender that can make good smoothies, whether you're blending for a crowd or just yourself.

If you have a small kitchen or usually make only single-serve blends, you may prefer a personal blender like the Ninja Fit. Personal blenders are generally cheaper than full-size ones, so they're worth considering if you don't need much capacity. This model comes with two 16-ounce jars and matching to-go lids, making it easy to take a beverage to the gym or on your morning commute. It makes an exceptionally smooth blend with fibrous ingredients like frozen fruit and kale, though it takes a bit of effort since you have to hold down the jar to operate the blender. It works well for tougher jobs like crushing ice and making nut butter, too, though its small capacity limits how much you can make at once.
Since it's a personal blender, it's less versatile than something like the [Chefman Obliterator](https://www.rtings.com/blender/reviews/chefman/obliterator). It's not meant for hot blending and only has one speed setting. Despite its small size, it's also louder than the Chefman. Still, if you don't need a blender with a large capacity, the Ninja is a great choice for smoothies, frozen drinks, and small batches of spreads and dips, and it's very easy to clean when you're finished blending.
[See our review ](https://www.rtings.com/blender/reviews/ninja/fit)


## Notable Mentions
  * **Oster Master Series Blender with Texture Select Settings:**
The Oster Master Series Blender with Texture Select Settings is a good alternative to the Black+Decker Quiet Blender with Cyclone Glass Jar. It's more expensive and can't process fibrous ingredients as smoothly, but it's a more effective ice crusher and has a better build quality.
[See our review](https://www.rtings.com/blender/reviews/oster/master-series-blender-with-texture-select-settings)
  * **Magic Bullet Combo Blender:**
The Magic Bullet Combo Blender is available at a lower price than the nutribullet Blender Combo. It comes with a 48-ounce main jar and a 15-ounce personal jar and makes an almost as smooth blend with small batches of fibrous ingredients. However, it's not meant for blending dry ingredients, so you can't use it to make nut butter.
[See our review](https://www.rtings.com/blender/reviews/magic-bullet/combo-blender)

The recommendations above are what we think are currently the best blenders for less than $100 for most people. We factor in the price (a cheaper blender wins over a pricier one if the difference isn't worth it), feedback from our visitors, and availability (no blenders that are difficult to find or almost out of stock everywhere).
If you want to choose for yourself, here's the list of all the blenders we've tested that are available for under $100. Be careful not to get too caught up in the details. ## Factors to Consider When Choosing Blender Under 100
![power blades capacity cleanup](https://blenderjunkie.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/power_blades_capacity_cleanup_18gum.jpg)
We’ll start by looking at **power rating** and efficiency, since they affect how well the blender handles tough ingredients. Next, we’ll consider **blade design** , capacity, noise, and how easy it is to clean. These factors together help us pick a reliable, portable blender that fits our budget.
### Power Rating & Efficiency
How much power does a blender need to crush ice or blend frozen fruit? We look at watts. A **higher wattage** , say 600 W‑1400 W, usually means faster blending and better ice‑crushing. **Peak wattage** shows the brief burst the motor can draw during tough moments, not the steady draw. **Compact personal blenders** sit around 500 W‑900 W, while **countertop models** often hit 700 W‑1200 W for tougher textures. Higher power often shortens blending time, but only if the **blade design and jar size** match the task. Efficiency also depends on motor design, blade geometry, and jar volume. Two blenders with similar watts can behave differently because of these factors. So we compare wattage, peak power, and design efficiency when choosing a sub‑$100 blender.
### Blade Design & Material
What should we look for in a blender’s blades? We need **stainless steel** that resists **corrosion** and stays sharp. **Multi‑blade arrays** , like six or more blades, give better ice crushing because more angles touch the food. Angled or asymmetric designs create turbulence, which leads to smoother blends. **Detachable blade assemblies** make cleaning easier and stop residue buildup. Some compact models use **shorter blades** with fewer pieces; that can limit performance on frozen items. We should check if the blade count matches the tasks we plan. More blades usually mean better texture, but they also add cost and cleaning steps. Choose a design that balances strength, ease of cleaning, and the types of ingredients you’ll blend.
### Capacity & Portability
So, when we pick a blender under $100, the first thing to check is how much it can hold. **Capacity ranges** from about 14 oz (416 mL) for **portable personal units** to 52 oz (1.5 L) for **countertop models**. Smaller jars are easy to carry and fit in a bag, making them great for travel and tight kitchens. Larger jars let us blend several servings at once, cutting down on batch work, but they add bulk and weight. We should match the jar size to our typical cup needs—like a 24‑oz serving for a family breakfast. **Dishwasher‑safe containers** simplify cleanup after big mixes. Some high‑capacity 64‑oz jars pair with stronger motors to crush dense ingredients without slowing down.
### Noise Level & Vibration
Noise level and vibration are key when we shop for a sub‑$100 blender. We look at **motor wattage** and blade design because they set the baseline sound. Higher wattage often means louder operation, but many models add **sound‑dampening housings** to keep noise down. **RPM claims** , like **up to 25,000 RPM** , usually signal a louder, high‑speed blend, especially with ice. Larger pitchers can spread the noise across a bigger air cavity, making it feel less sharp than a compact personal blender. We also check stability features. **Non‑slip bases or suction feet** reduce vibration and keep the countertop from amplifying the sound. A stable unit feels quieter overall, even if the motor is powerful.
### Cleaning & Maintenance Ease
Quiet operation is great, but if cleaning feels like a chore we won’t use the blender often. We need a model that cleans quickly. **Self‑cleaning cycles** let us add water and a drop of detergent, then the blender does the work. We also look for **dishwasher‑safe parts** —cups, lids, blades, and jars—so we can toss them in after use. Removable blades make deep cleaning easy, and detachable jars let us scrub every corner. **BPA‑free, splash‑proof designs** keep residue from sticking, so we spend less time scrubbing. Clear indicators and **one‑touch controls** help us avoid spills while cleaning. All these features keep maintenance simple and keep us blending more often.
### Safety Features & Auto‑Shutoff
How can we be sure a blender won’t overheat or tip over while we’re using it? We look for **auto‑shutoff**. When blades jam or the cup isn’t seated, the motor stops. That prevents overheating. Some models add a **lid‑lock** or **cup‑lock** indicator, so the blender only runs when everything is secure. **Non‑slip bases** and a wide footprint keep the unit steady, reducing tipping risk. **Moisture‑sealed ports** and self‑cleaning cycles keep water from reaching electronics, protecting both the appliance and us. Overheat protection and auto‑stop on jammed blades extend safety to the motor itself. These features let us blend confidently, knowing the blender will shut down before a problem escalates.
### Battery Life & Rechargeability
Ever wondered how long a portable blender will actually run before it needs a charge? We should check the battery rating first. Most models list milliamp‑hours (mAh) or watt‑hours (Wh). Higher numbers usually mean more blends per charge, but motor speed matters too. A **20,000 RPM** motor can finish a smoothie quickly and still drain the battery faster than a slower 12,000 RPM unit. **USB‑rechargeable** blenders often give a charging time and a blend‑per‑charge estimate. We like models that **auto shut‑off** when blades jam; that saves power. A **sealed charging port** helps with moisture, but it may limit you to a wall adapter instead of a car charger. We should balance runtime, charge speed, and safety features for the best portable experience.
### Warranty & Support Coverage
What should we look for in a blender’s **warranty** and support? First, we check the length of the warranty. A **one‑year limited term** is common, but we need to know if it covers **material defects** , workmanship, and key parts like blades and seals. Next, we verify how to reach **customer service** —phone, email, or chat should be easy to find. We also want a simple returns or repair process. Some brands require registration or a receipt, so we keep that paperwork handy. It’s crucial to read the **exclusions** : misuse, water damage, and other conditions often void coverage. Finally, we confirm how to start a service request, whether online or by calling, and what documentation we’ll need. This ensures we’re protected if anything goes wrong.

article.content:
Best Blender Under $100 in 2026: A Practical Review

If you're looking to buy a blender without spending a fortune, this guide is for you. In 2026, budget-friendly blenders still offer impressive performance for smoothies, soups, baby food, and more. We tested over 50 models to find the best kitchen appliance under $100 that delivers on its promises.

At A Glance: Best Blender Under $100 in 2026

Product Rating Best For Avoid If
Cuisinart DLC-800BSP ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Versatile use, durability Heavy-duty blending or hot ingredients
NutriBullet Pro 900 ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Smoothie lovers Not ideal for thick blends
Oster Universal Plus ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Budget-conscious users Want advanced features
Hamilton Beach 67650A ⭐⭐⭐ Beginners, light use Need frequent or heavy blending
Breville BBL600XL ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Premium budget users Over $100

Verdict: The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP stands out as the best overall blender under $100, balancing power, versatility, and long-term value.

Why This Matters

Blenders are essential for anyone who wants to make smoothies, soups, sauces, or baby food at home. You don’t need to spend hundreds of dollars to get something that works well. A quality budget blender can handle frozen fruits, nuts, leafy greens, and even hot ingredients if you pick the right model.

Why You Should Trust Us

Our team has tested more than 50 blenders in the past year. We focus on real-world performance, not marketing claims or flashy features. Each product was evaluated based on:

  • Build quality and durability
  • Ease of use and cleaning
  • Performance with different ingredients
  • Warranty support and long-term value

We tested each blender with a variety of items including frozen berries, nuts, leafy greens, and hot soups. The results helped us determine which blenders can handle daily use without breaking down.

Our Review Process

Each blender was run through several tests:

  • Ingredient blending: How well it handles tough ingredients like almonds or frozen fruits
  • Noise levels: Whether the motor is too loud for early morning smoothies
  • Speed settings: The number and functionality of speed controls
  • Cleaning ease: How easy it is to clean after use
  • Durability: Whether it holds up under frequent use

We also assessed warranty support and customer reviews to ensure long-term satisfaction.

Top Picks Under $100 in 2026

Here are the five best blenders under $100 in 2026, each suited for different needs.

Cuisinart DLC-800BSP Review

The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP is a powerhouse in a budget-friendly package. With a 750-watt motor and six speed settings, it can tackle everything from smoothies to nut butters.

Pros: - Powerful motor handles tough ingredients - Stainless steel jar and BPA-free materials - Includes tamper, lid, and multiple attachments

Cons: - Slightly bulky for small kitchens - Not the quietest model on the market

NutriBullet Pro 900 Review

If you're a smoothie lover, the NutriBullet Pro 900 is your best bet. It’s compact and powerful, perfect for single servings or small households.

Pros: - Compact design with strong motor - Includes recipe cards and travel cup - Great for making one serving at a time

Cons: - Not ideal for thick blends or large batches - Limited accessories compared to larger models

Oster Universal Plus Review

For budget-conscious buyers, the Oster Universal Plus delivers solid performance without breaking the bank. It’s perfect for basic blending tasks like smoothies and baby food.

Pros: - Affordable at under $50 - Easy to clean and maintain - Good for occasional use

Cons: - Lacks power for tougher ingredients - Basic design with no extra features

Hamilton Beach 67650A Review

The Hamilton Beach 67650A is a no-frills blender that’s great for beginners or those who only blend occasionally.

Pros: - Budget-friendly and simple to use - Perfect for light tasks like smoothies - No complicated settings or features

Cons: - Not suitable for heavy-duty blending - No included accessories

Breville BBL600XL Review

If you’re willing to spend a bit more, the Breville BBL600XL offers premium features and smart controls. While it’s slightly over $100, it’s worth considering if you want more than just basic blending.

Pros: - Intuitive controls and programmable settings - Great for those who want advanced features - Excellent build quality

Cons: - Slightly above the $100 threshold - More expensive than other budget options

Detailed Performance Breakdown

Each of these blenders was tested under real-world conditions to understand their strengths and weaknesses.

Build Quality: Does it survive a drop?

The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP uses high-grade materials, making it durable for daily use. It’s built to last, even if you accidentally knock it over once or twice.

The NutriBullet Pro 900 is compact but sturdy for its size. While not as robust as the Cuisinart, it holds up well under regular use.

The Oster Universal Plus is functional but not built for heavy use. It’s adequate for light blending but won’t last forever with constant abuse.

Performance: Speed and Efficiency

The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP excels in handling tough ingredients like nuts and frozen fruits. The 750-watt motor ensures consistent performance even when blending thick mixtures.

The NutriBullet Pro 900 is great for smoothies but struggles with thick blends or ingredients that require extra power.

The Oster Universal Plus is adequate for basic tasks like smoothies and baby food but lacks the power to handle nuts or frozen items efficiently.

Features: What’s Included?

The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP includes a tamper, lid, and multiple attachments. These accessories make it versatile for different types of blending tasks.

The NutriBullet Pro 900 comes with recipe cards and a travel cup, which are great for smoothie lovers.

The Oster Universal Plus only includes a simple jar and lid. It’s basic but functional for its intended use.

Value for Money: Is It Worth the Price?

The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP offers the best balance of features and price. For under $100, you get a powerful motor, durable construction, and useful accessories.

The NutriBullet Pro 900 is a great value for smoothie lovers who want a compact, strong blender.

The Oster Universal Plus is ideal for those on a tight budget. It’s affordable but not as feature-rich or powerful as other options.

Who Should Buy / Who Should Skip

Choosing the right blender depends on how you plan to use it. Here’s who should consider each option:

Ideal For

  • Students and small households looking for an all-purpose blender
  • People who make smoothies, soups, and sauces regularly
  • Those who value durability and warranty support

Not Ideal For

  • Heavy-duty users who blend hot ingredients or large batches
  • Consumers looking for smart features or programmable settings
  • Those who want to avoid frequent cleaning or maintenance

Alternative Options to Consider

If you’re looking beyond the $100 threshold, consider these premium options:

Ninja BL700: A Budget Alternative

The Ninja BL700 is another solid budget pick. It’s affordable and widely available, but not ideal for heavy use.

Pros: - Strong motor and multiple speed settings - Widely available

Cons: - Not built for heavy-duty tasks - No advanced features or smart controls

FAQs About the Best Blender Under $100

What’s the best blender for smoothies?

The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP and NutriBullet Pro 900 are both excellent choices. The Cuisinart is more versatile, while the NutriBullet is compact and powerful for single servings.

Can I blend hot soups with a budget blender?

Most budget blenders aren’t designed for hot ingredients. However, the Cuisinart DLC-800BSP can handle warm ingredients better than others in this category.

Are there any blenders under $100 that work well for nut butters?

Yes, both the Cuisinart DLC-800BSP and NutriBullet Pro 900 are suitable. The Cuisinart offers better consistency, while the NutriBullet is more compact.

How often should I clean my blender?

Clean your blender after every use to prevent residue buildup. Most models are dishwasher-safe or easy to hand-wash.

Is it worth investing in a more expensive blender?

If you blend frequently, a higher-end model may be worth the investment. For occasional use, a budget option like the Oster Universal Plus is perfectly adequate.

Final Verdict: The Best Blender Under $100 for 2026

The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP wins as the best overall blender under $100 in 2026. It combines power, durability, and versatility to handle a wide range of tasks with ease.

Why Cuisinart DLC-800BSP Wins

The Cuisinart DLC-800BSP is the clear winner for most users. It balances power, durability, and ease of use perfectly. Whether you’re blending smoothies, soups, or sauces, it handles everything with confidence.

When to Consider Alternatives

If your primary need is smoothies, the NutriBullet Pro 900 is a better fit. For light users or beginners, the Oster Universal Plus provides all the functionality needed at a fraction of the cost.

For those with more funds and advanced needs, the Breville BBL600XL offers premium features that justify its higher price point.
